cau_units.title: Download and Registration
test_steps: The user should be able to download the mobile application free of charge through an application store and subsequently complete the registration process by providing a user name, password, and e-mail address. The user has a compatible mobile handset with an active Internet connection and access to a supported application store.

Open the application store on the mobile device. 2. Search for and locate the Amazing Lunch Indicator application. 3. Download and install the application at no cost. 4. Launch the application. 5. Select 'Not registered? Click here' on the login page. 6. Provide the required registration fields and submit.
cau_units.expected_result: The application is available for free download. The registration form accepts the provided information and confirms account creation.
cau_units.actual_result: The application was successfully downloaded at no cost. Registration was completed without errors by all testers.
cau_units.tester_observations: No issues were reported. Testers found the registration flow straightforward.

linked_crus.action: A user should be able to download the mobile application through either an application store or similar service on the mobile phone.
The application should be free to download.
Given that a user has downloaded the mobile application, then the user should be able to register through the mobile application.
The user must provide user-name, password and e-mail address.
The user can choose to provide a regularly used phone number.

cau_units.title: User Log-In and Automatic Login
cau_units.description: A registered user should be able to log in to the mobile application. Login credentials should be stored on the device so that subsequent launches do not require re-entry of credentials.
preconditions: The user has successfully registered (FR3 passed).
test_steps: Launch the application and log in with valid credentials. 2. Close the application completely. 3. Relaunch the application and observe behaviour.
cau_units.expected_result: Login succeeds on the first attempt. On relaunch, the user is taken directly to the search page without being prompted to log in again.
cau_units.actual_result: Login succeeded for all testers. Automatic login on relaunch functioned correctly on all tested handsets.
cau_units.tester_observations: Testers appreciated the automatic login feature and noted it significantly reduced friction for repeat use.

linked_crus.action: Given that a user has registered, then the user should be able to log in to the mobile application.
The log-in information will be stored on the phone and in the future the user should be logged in automatically.

linked_test_cases.test_title: User logs in successfully with valid credentials
Invalid password prevents user log-in
User logs in with empty password
User registration and log-in integration test
User logs in successfully and is automatically logged in on subsequent visits
Invalid login credentials result in error message and no automatic log-in
User log-in with empty password field results in error message and no automatic log-in
User log-in integrates with restaurant search feature

coverage.summary: UAT PASS. 2 CRU(s) and 8 test case(s) linked.

cau_units.title: Search Functionality
cau_units.description: A logged-in user should be able to search for restaurants using one or more of the available search options: price, destination, restaurant type, specific dish, and free-text search.
preconditions: The user is logged in and the device has an active Internet and GPS connection.
test_steps: From the search page, select each search option individually and perform a search. 2. Combine multiple search options in a single search and verify results.
Use the free-text search field with restaurant name, address, and menu terms.
cau_units.expected_result: Each search option returns relevant results. Combined searches correctly apply all selected criteria simultaneously.
cau_units.actual_result: Price, destination, restaurant type, and dish searches returned correct results. Free-text search returned accurate results for restaurant name and address but did not always match menu item terms.
cau_units.tester_observations: Testers found the free-text search useful but were surprised that searching for a specific dish name in the free-text field did not consistently return the same results as the dedicated dish search option. A change request has been raised; see CR-01 in Section 4.2.

linked_requirements.title: Mobile application - Search by restaurant type
Mobile application - Search by specific dish

linked_crus.action: Given that a user is logged in to the mobile application, then the first page that is shown should be the search page.
The user should be able to search for a restaurant, according to several search options.
There should also be a free- text search option.
A user should be able to select multiple search options in one search.
A user should be able to input a maximum and a minimum price range.
A user should be able to input a maximum and a minimum distance, according to his/her position.
The user should be able to input a higher or lower maximum distance and a higher minimum distance than set by default.
A user should be able to select a restaurant type in a given list as input.
A user should be able to select a specific dish in a given list as input.
A user should be able to conduct a search by providing either restaurant name, restaurant description, restaurant address, restaurant type or restaurant menu in the free-text search field.

cau_units.title: Search Results – Map View
cau_units.description: Search results should be displayed on a map with restaurant pins, a maximum of one hundred results, a default zoom level, and a filtering menu button.
preconditions: A search has been performed that returns at least one result.
cau_units.expected_result: 1. Perform a search by destination. 2. Observe the map view. 3. Tap a pin and verify the information link is accessible. 4. Tap the filtering button and verify the filtering menu appears. Restaurant locations appear as pins on the map. The user's location is visible. Tapping a pin reveals an information link. The filtering button opens the filtering menu.
cau_units.actual_result: All elements functioned as specified. Map loaded correctly with GPS position shown. Filtering menu was accessible with a single tap.
cau_units.tester_observations: Testers commented positively on the clarity of pin placement. One tester noted that the default zoom level felt slightly too close when there were many nearby restaurants; this has been noted as an enhancement suggestion.

linked_requirements.title: Mobile application - Search result in a map view
Usage of the result in the map view

linked_crus.action:  Search results can be viewed on a map.
 A specific pin will represent a specific restaurant location.
On each pin there should be an information link.
 There should be maximally 100 results displayed.
The map view should have a default zoom.
 The map view should include a button that, when selected, should display different filtering options in a filtering menu.
The results displayed in the map view should be user friendly and easy to understand.
Selecting a pin on the map should only take one click.

cau_units.title: Search Results – List View
cau_units.description: Search results should be displayed in a list with restaurant name, distance, type of food, average price, a short description, a link to the restaurant website, and an information link. Results should be scrollable and sortable.
preconditions: A search by price has been performed.
cau_units.actual_result: 1. Perform a search by price and verify the list view appears by default. 2. Scroll through the results. 3. Use the sorting header to sort by each available option. 4. Verify each list item includes all required fields. List view displays correctly with all required fields per item. Sorting functions correctly for each option. The list is scrollable when there are more results than fit on screen. List view functioned as specified. All required data fields were present. Sorting by price and distance operated correctly. Sorting by restaurant name was alphabetically correct.
cau_units.tester_observations: No significant issues. Testers requested that the restaurant website link open in a browser rather than within the application. This has been raised as CR-02 in Section 4.2. FR19 (Sorting Results) coverage confirmed in this test case.

linked_requirements.title: Mobile application - Search result in a list view
Usage of the result in the list view

linked_crus.action:  Search results can be viewed in a list.

Each element should include the restaurant name, telephone number, type of food, distance according to the user’s position, average price, a short two-line description, a link to the restaurant’s web-page and an information link.
 There should be maximally 100 results displayed.
If the result contains more restaurants than what can be displayed on the screen at one time, the user should be able to scroll through them.
 When searching by price the restaurants should be sorted according to the following order: 1.
specific dish  When searching by a search option, other than price, the restaurants should be sorted according to the following order: 1.
specific dish  The list view should include a header with different selectable sorting options.
 The list view should include a button that, when selected, should display different filtering options in a filtering menu.
When viewing the results in a list, a user should be able to sort the results according to price, distance, restaurant type, specific dish or restaurant name.
 When sorting by restaurant name, specific dish or restaurant type the results should be ordered alphabetically.
 When sorting by price the results should be ordered from cheapest to most expensive.
 When sorting by distance the results should be ordered from closets to furthest distance according to the user’s position.
The results displayed in the list view should be user friendly and easy to understand.
Selecting an element in the result list should only take one click.

cau_units.title: Navigation to Restaurant
cau_units.description: A user should be able to select a restaurant from either the map or list view and be navigated to it via the mobile GPS navigator. Upon arrival, the user should be able to return to the search page.
preconditions: Search results are displayed. GPS Navigator is installed on the device.
test_steps: Select a restaurant pin on the map view. 2. Confirm navigation handoff to the GPS Navigator. 3. Complete navigation and return to the ALI search page.
cau_units.expected_result: The GPS Navigator receives the destination and begins navigation. After arrival, the back navigation to the ALI search page is functional.
cau_units.actual_result: Navigation handoff worked correctly on Android devices. On one iOS device, the handoff to the GPS Navigator required an additional confirmation step not anticipated by the specification.
cau_units.tester_observations: The variation in behaviour across platforms is linked to the GPS interface constraint noted in SRS Section 2.4. A change request CR-03 has been raised to document platform-specific behaviour explicitly.

linked_crus.action: A user should be able to select a pin on a map or an element on a list.
When a selection is made, the location of the restaurant should be sent to the mobile phone’s GPS-navigation program.
The user should then be navigated to the destination.
When the destination is reached, a user should be able to go back to the search page on the mobile application.

cau_units.title: Filtering Results
cau_units.description: A user should be able to filter existing search results by maximum distance, maximum price, restaurant type, and specific dish without issuing a new search query.
preconditions: Search results are displayed in either map or list view.
test_steps: Open the filtering menu. 2. Adjust each filter option and apply. 3. Verify that only existing results are refined, and no new database query is issued.
cau_units.expected_result: Results update immediately to reflect the applied filters. No loading indicator appears, confirming no new server request is made.
cau_units.actual_result: Filtering operated on existing results without issuing new server requests. Results updated correctly in both views.
cau_units.tester_observations: Testers found the filtering feature very useful when the initial search returned a large number of results.

linked_crus.action: When viewing the results in a list or a map, a user should be able to filter the results in a filtering menu.

cau_units.title: Profile Page
cau_units.description: The user should have a profile page allowing editing of e-mail address, phone number, and password, as well as selection of the application language from Swedish, English, Spanish, and French.
preconditions: The user is logged in.
test_steps: Navigate to the profile page using the P icon. 2. Edit each available field and save. 3. Change the language and verify the interface updates accordingly.
cau_units.expected_result: Profile information updates correctly. The selected language is applied across the entire mobile application interface.
cau_units.actual_result: Profile editing worked correctly. Language change was applied to all user-facing text on the mobile application.
cau_units.tester_observations: No issues were reported.

linked_crus.action: On the mobile application, a user should have a profile page.
On the profile page a user can edit his/her information, which includes the password, e-mail address and phone number.
A user should also be able to choose what language the mobile application should be set to.

cau_units.title: Prominent Search Feature
cau_units.description: The search feature should be prominent and easy to find for the user.
preconditions: The user is logged in to the mobile application.
test_steps: Launch the mobile application and log in. 2. Observe the initial screen presented to the user. 3. Assess whether the search feature is the primary visible element without requiring additional navigation.
cau_units.expected_result: The search page is the default view after login and the search feature is immediately visible and prominent without scrolling or additional navigation.
cau_units.actual_result: The search page was presented as the default view upon login. The search input area and available search criteria were clearly presented at the top of the screen and were immediately visible to all testers.
cau_units.tester_observations: All testers were able to locate and begin using the search feature without any guidance. No issues were reported.

linked_crus.action: The search feature should be prominent and easy to find for the user.

linked_test_cases.test_title: Invalid search input
Search integration with dashboard

coverage.summary: UAT PASS. 1 CRU(s) and 2 test case(s) linked.

cau_units.title: Ease of Use of Search Options
cau_units.description: The different search options should be evident, simple, and easy to understand.
preconditions: The user is logged in and the search page is visible.
test_steps: Without any prior instruction, ask testers to identify all available search options on the search page. 2. Ask testers to perform a search using each individual search option. 3. Collect feedback on the clarity and ease of understanding of each option.
cau_units.expected_result: All testers can identify the available search options without assistance and successfully initiate a search using each option on their first attempt.
cau_units.actual_result: All testers correctly identified the price, destination, restaurant type, dish, and free-text search options without instruction. Each search option was used successfully on the first attempt.
cau_units.tester_observations: Testers found the labelling of each search option clear and self-explanatory. No confusion was observed between the different search criteria.

linked_crus.action: The different search options should be evident, simple and easy to understand.

linked_test_cases.test_title: Invalid search criteria
Search results integration with dashboard

coverage.summary: UAT PASS. 1 CRU(s) and 2 test case(s) linked.

cau_units.title: Ease of Use of the Information Link
cau_units.description: The information link should be prominent and it should be evident that it is a usable link. Selecting the information link should only take one click.
preconditions: A search has been performed and results are displayed in either list or map view.
test_steps: Observe the information link displayed alongside each result item in the list view. 2. Observe the information link displayed next to each pin in the map view.
Tap the information link once and verify that the restaurant information page is displayed. 4. Confirm that no additional tap or confirmation step is required.
cau_units.expected_result: The information link is clearly distinguishable as a tappable element. A single tap opens the restaurant information page in all cases.
cau_units.actual_result: The information link was visually distinct and all testers identified it as interactive without prompting. A single tap consistently opened the information page in both list and map views.
cau_units.tester_observations: No issues were reported. Testers considered the information link clearly labelled and intuitively positioned within both result views. FR11 (Information Link) coverage confirmed.

linked_requirements.title: Mobile application - Selecting the information link

linked_crus.action: A user should be able to select the information link, which is included on all result items.

The link will direct the user to an information page, which includes a picture of the restaurant, the restaurant name, address, phone number, e-mail address, type of food, average price, restaurant description and a menu with name, description and price of the different dishes.
The information link should be prominent and it should be evident that it is a usable link.
Selecting the information link should only take one click.

cau_units.title: System Response Time
cau_units.description: The response time of a search should be no more than two seconds in all cases.
test_steps: The device has an active Internet and GPS connection. The test environment replicates the intended production infrastructure.

Perform a series of one thousand searches across varying search options, locations, and result set sizes during the testing period. 2. Measure the elapsed time from submission of the search request to the display of results. 3. Record the response time for each search.
cau_units.expected_result: One hundred percent of searches return results within two seconds. The desired target is for one hundred percent of searches to complete within one second.
cau_units.actual_result: Measurements obtained over the testing period showed that all searches completed within the two-second threshold. The majority of searches resolved within one second under normal network conditions.
cau_units.tester_observations: A small number of testers noted occasional brief delays during peak concurrent usage, though all measured results remained within the two-second MUST threshold. Continued optimisation is recommended.

linked_test_cases.test_title: Response Time: Search Restaurants by Distance and Price

coverage.summary: UAT PASS. 1 CRU(s) and 4 test case(s) linked.

cau_units.title: System Dependability
cau_units.description: If the system loses the connection to the Internet or to the GPS device, or if the system receives unexpected input, the user should be informed.
preconditions: The mobile application is running and the user is logged in.
cau_units.actual_result: 1. Disable the Internet connection on the device while the application is active and observe the application response. 2. Disable the GPS signal and observe the application response. 3. Re-enable connectivity and verify recovery. 4. Submit unexpected or malformed input in search fields and observe the response. In all three scenarios, the application notifies the user clearly of the fault condition. The application does not crash or produce an unhandled error. This behaviour is observed in one hundred percent of cases. Loss of Internet connection triggered an appropriate notification to the user in all tested cases. Loss of GPS signal was detected and communicated correctly. Malformed inputs were handled gracefully with informative user messages.
cau_units.tester_observations: Testers confirmed that error notifications were clear and allowed them to understand the cause of the interruption. Recovery after restoring connectivity was smooth and required no restart of the application.

inform user about strange input

cau_units.title: Application Hard Drive Space Usage
cau_units.description: The installed application should occupy no more than twenty megabytes of storage on the mobile device.
preconditions: The mobile application is fully installed on the test handset.
test_steps: Navigate to the device storage settings after installation. 2. Record the storage space occupied by the Amazing Lunch Indicator application. 3. Verify the recorded value against the specified thresholds.
cau_units.expected_result: The installed application occupies no more than twenty megabytes. The planned target is no more than fifteen megabytes, with a desired target of ten megabytes.
cau_units.actual_result: The installed application size was measured at approximately twelve megabytes across all test handsets, satisfying both the MUST threshold and the PLAN target.
cau_units.tester_observations: No issues were reported. The application footprint was considered acceptable by all testers.

Hard drive space: Invalid input handling for restaurant management

coverage.summary: UAT PASS. 1 CRU(s) and 4 test case(s) linked.

cau_units.title: Application Memory Usage
cau_units.actual_result: The amount of operating system memory occupied by the application while running should not exceed twenty megabytes. The mobile application is running on the test handset with no other applications consuming significant memory. 1. Launch the mobile application and perform a series of typical user interactions including search, map view, list view, and navigation handoff. 2. Monitor memory consumption via the device performance log throughout the session. 3. Record peak and average memory usage. Peak memory consumption does not exceed twenty megabytes at any point. The planned target is no more than sixteen megabytes, with a desired target of ten megabytes. Memory consumption was observed to peak at approximately fourteen megabytes during intensive operations such as loading the map view with a full result set. This satisfies both the MUST and PLAN thresholds.
cau_units.tester_observations: No memory-related crashes or performance degradation were observed during the test sessions. Memory usage remained stable across extended sessions.

cau_units.title: Download of Updated Application Releases
cau_units.status: NOT TESTED – SCHEDULED FOR NEXT RELEASE CYCLE
cau_units.actual_result: When an updated version of the mobile application is published to the application store, the user should be able to download and install the update through the same application store mechanism used for the initial installation, at no cost. The user has the Amazing Lunch Indicator installed. An updated version of the application has been published to the supported application store. 1. Open the application store on the mobile device. 2. Navigate to the Amazing Lunch Indicator listing or the updates section. 3. Initiate the update download. 4. Verify the update installs without error. 5. Launch the updated application and confirm the version number reflects the new release. 6. Verify that existing user credentials and preferences are preserved following the update. The updated application is available in the application store at no charge. The update installs without error. The user is not required to re-register. Previously stored login credentials and language preferences are retained. NOT TESTED in v1.1. No test environment simulating an application store update was available during the original UAT sessions. This test case is added in v1.2 and must be executed against the next application release.
cau_units.tester_observations: No tester observations available. This test case must be executed before sign-off on any future release that increments the application version number. The test team should coordinate with the application store to obtain a staged rollout environment for validation.

cau_units.title: Behaviour When Search Returns No Results
cau_units.status: NOT TESTED – MUST BE EXECUTED BEFORE NEXT RELEASE
cau_units.description: When a search query returns no matching restaurants, the application should inform the user clearly that no results were found and provide guidance on how to modify the search criteria.
preconditions: The user is logged in. The device has an active Internet and GPS connection.
cau_units.expected_result: 1. Perform a search using a combination of criteria that is known to return no results (e.g. a highly specific dish name in a location where no matching restaurant exists). 2. Observe the application response in both map view and list view. 3. Verify that an appropriate message is displayed to the user. 4. Verify that the application does not crash or display an unformatted empty state. 5. Confirm that the user is able to return to the search page and modify criteria without restarting the application. The application displays a clear, user-friendly message indicating that no restaurants matched the search criteria. Both map and list views handle the empty state gracefully without errors. The user is presented with an option or guidance to modify the search. The application remains fully functional after an empty result.
cau_units.actual_result: NOT TESTED in v1.1. The original UAT sessions did not include a scenario specifically designed to produce an empty result set. This test case is added in v1.2 and must be executed and signed off before the next release.
cau_units.tester_observations: No tester observations available. The project team should prepare a test dataset that guarantees an empty result for at least one search combination. The empty state message, if present, should be reviewed for clarity and consistency with the application's language selection setting.

linked_crus.action: If no match is found the user should be informed but kept on the search page in order to get the possibility to conduct a new search right away.

cau_units.title: Account Creation and Verification
cau_units.actual_result: A restaurant owner should be able to register on the web-portal by providing required information and subsequently receive a confirmation e-mail once the account has been verified by an administrator. The restaurant owner has access to the web-portal through a standard web browser. 1. Navigate to the web-portal. 2. Complete the registration form with all mandatory fields. 3. cau_units.title: Verifying Restaurant Owners
test_steps: An administrator should be able to verify or reject a restaurant owner registration. The restaurant owner should receive an appropriate notification e-mail in either case. A restaurant owner has submitted a registration request and is awaiting verification.
Log in as administrator. 2. Locate a pending restaurant owner registration. 3. Verify one registration and reject another. 4. Confirm that appropriate e-mails are sent in both cases.
cau_units.expected_result: Verified restaurant owners receive a confirmation e-mail and are able to log in. Rejected restaurant owners receive a rejection e-mail and cannot log in.
cau_units.actual_result: Both verification and rejection workflows functioned correctly. E-mail notifications were sent and received within acceptable timeframes.
cau_units.tester_observations: Administrators requested a consolidated view of all pending verifications rather than having to navigate to individual records. This has been captured as CR-06 in Section 4.2.

cau_units.title: Managing Restaurant Information
preconditions: An administrator should be able to add, edit, and delete information associated with individual restaurants within the system. The administrator is logged in. At least one restaurant record exists in the system.
cau_units.actual_result: 1. Navigate to the restaurant management section of the web-portal. 2. Add new information to an existing restaurant record. 3. Edit the information that was just added. 4. Delete the information and verify it is removed from the restaurant record. 5. Confirm the changes are reflected in the mobile application search results. Add, edit, and delete operations on restaurant information complete successfully. Changes are visible in the system immediately and are reflected in the mobile application after the next search. All three operations completed without error. Added and edited information appeared correctly in both the web-portal view and in the mobile application search results. Deleted information was removed from both interfaces.
cau_units.tester_observations: No significant issues were reported. Administrators noted that the restaurant information management interface was consistent in layout with the other administrative management screens, which aided navigation.

cau_units.title: Language Selection on the Web-Portal
cau_units.description: An administrator should be able to select a preferred language for the web-portal from Swedish, English, Spanish, and French.
preconditions: The administrator is logged in to the web-portal.
cau_units.actual_result: 1. Locate the language selection control on the web-portal. 2. Select each of the four available languages in turn: English, Swedish, French, and Spanish. 3. After each selection, verify that all visible text on the portal updates to the chosen language. 4. Confirm that no partial or untranslated text strings are displayed. The portal interface is fully rendered in the selected language immediately upon selection. All four languages function correctly without any untranslated text strings remaining. Language selection functioned correctly for all four languages. All portal text, including menu items, form labels, and button text, updated immediately upon language change. No untranslated strings were observed.
cau_units.tester_observations: No issues were reported. Administrators noted that the language selection behaviour was consistent with that available to restaurant owners, which ensured a familiar experience across both stakeholder groups.

cau_units.title: System Availability
cau_units.description: The average system availability, excluding network failures, should exceed ninety-eight percent of the time.
cau_units.actual_result: The system is deployed in a test environment that replicates the intended production configuration. Monitoring tooling is in place to record uptime and downtime events. 1. Monitor system availability continuously across a simulated usage period of one thousand hours. 2. Record all instances of system unavailability that are attributable to the application rather than to network or GPS infrastructure. 3. Calculate the overall availability percentage. System availability exceeds ninety-eight percent across the one thousand hour measurement period. The planned target is above ninety-nine percent, with a desired target of one hundred percent. System availability measured at 99.2 percent across the observation period, satisfying both the MUST and PLAN thresholds. The two downtime events recorded were attributable to scheduled maintenance windows.
cau_units.tester_observations: No unexpected outages were observed. The team noted that the scheduled maintenance events should be communicated to users in advance through an in-application notification.
